definition: A time-space tradeoff is the deliberate choice to spend more of one resource (memory or compute) to save the other. Most practical algorithmic decisions — caching, memoization, indexing, batching, precomputation — are explicit positions on this tradeoff curve.
layman: If you keep a phone book at home, you spend shelf space to save time per call. If you don't, you save shelf space but pay time looking each number up. Engineering is constantly choosing where on that line to sit.
content:
## Time-space tradeoffs

### The fundamental tension

Every algorithm has a footprint in two axes — **time** (operations) and **space** (memory). Sometimes we can move along the curve: pay a bit more memory and the algorithm runs much faster, or pay a bit more time and use much less memory.

Knowing which direction to move requires:

- Knowing how much of each you have available (RAM, latency budget, cost).
- Knowing how the algorithm's complexity scales in each axis.
- Knowing the access pattern (one-off vs many queries).

### Common 'pay memory to save time' moves

| Move | Time saved | Extra memory |
|------|------------|--------------|
| **Hash table** instead of list scan | O(n) → O(1) avg | O(n) |
| **Memoization** of expensive recursion | O(2ⁿ) → O(n) | O(n) memo |
| **Prefix sums** for range queries | O(n) → O(1) per query | O(n) |
| **Sort once, binary-search many** | O(n) → O(log n) per query | O(n log n) once (sort) |
| **Precomputed lookup table** | O(f(n)) per query → O(1) | O(table size) |
| **Index / inverted index** | O(n) corpus scan → O(1) lookup | O(n) index |
| **Caching responses** | API call → memory hit | O(cache size) |

### Common 'pay time to save memory' moves

| Move | Memory saved | Extra time |
|------|--------------|------------|
| **Streaming / iterators** | O(n) → O(1) | overlap reading with processing |
| **Rolling rows in DP** | O(m·n) → O(n) | same |
| **Recompute instead of cache** | cache → 0 | re-derive on each access |
| **External sort / merge** | O(n) → O(buffer) | extra disk I/O |
| **Bit packing** | O(n bytes) → O(n bits) | masking and shifting |
| **Sparse representation** | O(n²) → O(nnz) | indirect lookups |

### Big-O isn't the whole story

Big-O hides constants, but constants matter when you sit on the tradeoff curve:

- A hash table is O(1) average but has memory and hashing overhead — for very small n, a linear scan over a tight cache-friendly list beats it.
- A DP table can be theoretically O(n²) but the cache miss pattern makes it 10× slower than expected unless you tile it.
- 'Free' memory may not actually be free (eviction pressure, GC, allocator fragmentation).

### Decision rubric

1. **What's the bottleneck right now — latency, throughput, RAM, cost?**
2. **What's the access pattern — one-off, many queries, streaming?**
3. **What's the budget on each axis** (max RAM, p99 latency, $/query)?
4. **Pick the move that buys you the scarcer resource.**

### Why this matters for Gen AI

- **Vector indexes (HNSW, IVF, PQ)** are massive time-saving moves at the cost of GB of index memory.
- **KV-cache** in LLM inference is a textbook time-saving cache that costs memory proportional to context length.
- **Quantization (int8, int4)** is paying *quality* and time for memory savings — same shape of tradeoff in another axis.
- **Prompt caching** at the API layer turns 'recompute the prefix' into 'reload it' — pays storage for latency.
- **Streaming generation** avoids materializing the whole completion — saves memory at the cost of more orchestration code.
- **DP table compression (rolling rows)** for edit-distance-style eval pipelines drops O(m·n) memory to O(n).

The best engineers don't fight the tradeoff — they place themselves at the right point on the curve for the system they're building.
keyPoints: Time-space tradeoff = consciously spending one resource to save the other.
Memoization, caching, hash tables, prefix sums, indexes are 'pay memory, save time' moves.
Streaming, rolling DP rows, recomputation, bit-packing are 'pay time, save memory' moves.
Big-O is necessary but not sufficient — constants, cache behavior and budgets matter.
Many Gen AI building blocks (KV-cache, vector indexes, quantization, prompt caching) are explicit positions on this tradeoff.
